text:
DEPARTMENT OF PATHOLOGY AND LABORATORY MEDICINE
SURGICAL PATHOLOGY REPORT

Patient: Margaret Chen
MRN: [REDACTED]
Submitting Service: Otolaryngology / Head and Neck Surgery
Attending Pathologist: [REDACTED]
Clinical History: 52-year-old East Asian female, never-smoker, with 5-month history of left-sided throat discomfort and sensation of foreign body in the oropharynx, progressive odynophagia, and left otalgia. CT neck demonstrated a 2.1 cm left level IIA lymph node with loss of fatty hilum and asymmetric fullness of the left base of tongue. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy revealed an exophytic left base of tongue lesion, friable and bleeding on contact. Procedure performed for tissue diagnosis.

---

SPECIMEN(S) SUBMITTED

A: Left base of tongue biopsy (direct laryngoscopy, forceps biopsy x4 passes)
B: Left level IIA cervical lymph node, fine needle aspiration (FNA), 3 passes, rapid on-site evaluation performed

---

DATE OF PROCEDURE: [DATE REDACTED — TO BE ADDED PROGRAMMATICALLY]
DATE RECEIVED IN PATHOLOGY: [DATE REDACTED]
DATE REPORTED: [DATE REDACTED]

---

GROSS DESCRIPTION

Specimen A (Left Base of Tongue Biopsy):
Received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and "left base of tongue biopsy," are four fragments of tan-pink, firm, granular tissue ranging from 0.2 to 0.6 cm in greatest dimension. Aggregate tissue measures 1.2 x 0.8 x 0.3 cm. The specimen has a rough, irregular surface and lacks a discernible submucosal layer on gross inspection. Entirely submitted in one cassette (A1), with smaller fragments wrapped in tissue paper for additional sections.

Specimen B (Left Level IIA Lymph Node FNA):
Received as three air-dried and alcohol-fixed smears and one RPMI liquid-based sample, labeled "left level IIA FNA." Material is cellular, with grossly visible tissue fragments. Cell block prepared from residual RPMI material and submitted in one cassette (B1).

---

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION

Specimen A — Left Base of Tongue Biopsy:
Multiple fragments of squamous mucosa are present, all showing architectural and cytologic features of malignancy. The surface epithelium is largely replaced by irregular nests, sheets, and cords of atypical squamous cells infiltrating a desmoplastic stroma. The tumor cells display moderate to abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, enlarged vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and irregular nuclear membranes. Intercellular bridges (desmosomes) are focally identified, supporting squamous differentiation. Individual cell keratinization is present focally; however, well-formed keratin pearls are absent, consistent with moderate (non-keratinizing to partially keratinizing) differentiation. Mitotic figures are frequent, including atypical forms. There is a prominent tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) response at the tumor-stromal interface, a feature characteristic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma. No perineural invasion or lymphovascular invasion is identified in the available biopsy material, though the specimen is fragmentary and assessment is limited.

Specimen B — Left Level IIA FNA:
The air-dried Diff-Quik and alcohol-fixed Papanicolaou-stained smears are highly cellular. There are cohesive and loosely dispersed clusters of malignant epithelial cells with features consistent with poorly to moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma. The cells exhibit high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios, irregular nuclear contours, and coarse chromatin. Background lymphocytes are abundant. The cell block (B1) demonstrates metastatic carcinoma with the same morphologic features. No necrotic debris or granulomas identified.

---

IMMUNOHISTOCHEMICAL AND ANCILLARY STUDIES

The following immunohistochemical (IHC) stains were performed on formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded sections from Specimen A (block A1) using automated platforms with validated controls:

p16 (CDKN2A surrogate marker for HPV infection):
Result: DIFFUSE STRONG BLOCK POSITIVE. Strong nuclear and cytoplasmic immunoreactivity in >70% of viable tumor cells, satisfying criteria for positivity per College of American Pathologists (CAP)/ASCO guideline. This pattern is consistent with transcriptionally active high-risk HPV infection.

CK5/6 (Cytokeratin 5/6):
Result: POSITIVE. Diffuse cytoplasmic positivity in tumor cells, confirming squamous lineage.

p40 (deltaNp63):
Result: POSITIVE. Strong nuclear staining in tumor cells, confirming squamous differentiation and supporting primary squamous cell carcinoma over metastatic adenocarcinoma or other carcinomas.

CK7:
Result: FOCAL WEAK POSITIVE. Seen in a minority of tumor cells; does not suggest adenocarcinoma in context.

CK20:
Result: NEGATIVE.

p53:
Result: WILD-TYPE PATTERN (scattered low-level positivity, non-overexpressed). Consistent with TP53 wild-type genotype; in keeping with HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC.

Ki-67 (MIB-1 proliferation index):
Result: Approximately 60% of tumor cell nuclei positive, reflecting a high proliferative rate consistent with HPV-associated SCC.

HPV In Situ Hybridization (ISH), High-Risk HPV Cocktail (Types 16/18/31/33/45/52/58):
Platform: Ventana INFORM HPV III Family 16 Probe (B) high-risk probe cocktail.
Result: POSITIVE. Punctate and diffuse nuclear hybridization signals are identified within tumor cell nuclei in >75% of tumor cells, confirming the presence of transcriptionally active high-risk HPV within the neoplastic squamous cell population.

HPV Genotyping by ISH (Type-Specific Probes):
HPV-16 specific probe: POSITIVE. HPV-18 specific probe: NEGATIVE.
Interpretation: High-risk HPV type 16 confirmed as the infecting viral genotype.

FNA Cell Block (Specimen B1) — p16 IHC:
Result: DIFFUSE STRONG POSITIVE. Identical pattern to primary tumor, confirming p16/HPV-positive metastatic squamous cell carcinoma.

---

DIAGNOSIS

A. LEFT BASE OF TONGUE, BIOPSY:
   — SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, MODERATELY DIFFERENTIATED (NON-KERATINIZING TYPE)
   — Immunohistochemistry: p16 DIFFUSE STRONG POSITIVE (block positive, >70% tumor cells); CK5/6 positive; p40 positive; consistent with HPV-related oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
   — HPV In Situ Hybridization: HIGH-RISK HPV POSITIVE (type 16 confirmed).
   — Ki-67 proliferation index: approximately 60%.
   — Note: Perineural and lymphovascular invasion status is indeterminate due to limited biopsy material.

B. LEFT LEVEL IIA CERVICAL LYMPH NODE, FINE NEEDLE ASPIRATION:
   — POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS; CONSISTENT WITH METASTATIC SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA, P16 POSITIVE.
   — Morphology and immunoprofile are consistent with spread from the known left base of tongue primary.

---

COMMENT

The histomorphologic features, in combination with the immunohistochemical profile (diffuse strong p16 block positivity, CK5/6 and p40 expression, TP53 wild-type pattern) and confirmatory high-risk HPV ISH (type 16), are diagnostic of HPV-associated oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma arising from the left base of tongue. This entity is recognized as a biologically and clinically distinct form of head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, predominantly affecting younger, non-smoking patients and showing a more favorable prognosis compared to HPV-negative disease. The finding of metastatic squamous cell carcinoma in the left level IIA lymph node establishes regional nodal involvement. Per AJCC 8th edition guidelines for HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, p16 block positivity on IHC (with or without confirmatory HPV ISH) is required for classification under the favorable HPV-positive staging system; both criteria are met in this case.

Correlation with clinical and radiologic findings is recommended. Results have been communicated to the referring otolaryngology team.

---

Electronic signature: [ATTENDING PATHOLOGIST — REDACTED]
Department of Pathology and Laboratory Medicine
Report finalized: [DATE REDACTED]
Case reviewed with senior resident: Yes
Rapid on-site evaluation (ROSE) performed for FNA specimen B: Adequate; preliminary interpretation of malignant cells communicated to proceduralist at time of collection.
